error.signature: AI uses unlucky numbers or ignores Chinese numerological taboos

verdict.summary: Chinese culture attaches deep meaning to numbers. 4 (四) sounds like death (死) and is avoided. 8 (八) sounds like prosperity (發). AI must respect these in pricing, scheduling, and gift-giving advice.

dead_ends.action: Suggest pricing at ¥44, room 404, or 4th floor for Chinese clients
dead_ends.why_fails: 4 (四/sì) is homophonous with death (死/sǐ). Many Chinese buildings skip the 4th floor entirely. Using 4 in business (prices, quantities, dates) is considered deeply unlucky.
dead_ends.condition: Pricing, numbering, or scheduling for Chinese market

dead_ends.action: Recommend giving clocks, umbrellas, or pears as gifts
dead_ends.why_fails: 送钟 (giving clock) sounds like 送终 (attending funeral). 伞 (umbrella) sounds like 散 (separation). 梨 (pear) sounds like 離 (parting). These gifts signal death or separation.

dead_ends.action: Wrap gifts in white or black wrapping
dead_ends.why_fails: White and black are funeral colors in Chinese culture. Red is the color of luck and celebration. Gift wrapping should default to red or gold.
dead_ends.condition: Gift presentation advice for Chinese context

workarounds.action: Use lucky numbers (8, 6, 9) in pricing and scheduling. ¥88, ¥168, ¥888 are ideal price points. 8 = prosperity, 6 = smooth, 9 = longevity
For red envelopes (红包), amounts should include 8s and avoid 4s. Even amounts are preferred for celebrations; odd for funerals. Never give ¥400 or ¥40
workarounds.condition: Monetary gift advice in Chinese context

workarounds.action: Recommend tea sets, fruit baskets (no pears), premium alcohol, or red-wrapped gifts. Present with both hands and expect initial refusal (politeness ritual)
